entry.title: Accelerate Insights with the new Quick Builder in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Data-driven decisions shouldn’t wait. The new Quick Builder in SAP Analytics Cloud optimized story reimagines how business users and analysts move from data to decisions - faster, guided, and with best practices built in. While exploring a table in an optimized story, Quick Builder provides a simple, guided experience that shortens the path to value.

 

What is the Quick Builder for optimized stories

Quick Builder is a simplified builder panel for the table widget that you can launch in view mode from the table’s action menu or via script. It enables out-of-the-box, lightweight data exploration within stories:

  • Quickly change which dimensions and measures (or accounts) appear in the table.
  • Swap axes and rearrange objects across rows and columns.
  • Add or remove objects without leaving the story.
  • Modify dimension settings such as hierarchy selection and display options.
  • Define settings for rows and columns directly in context.
  • Automatically adapt to each table’s data source. When you navigate to another table on the story page that uses a different data model, Quick Builder updates to show the corresponding objects for that model, keeping exploration fluid without manual reconfiguration.

 

How to launch Quick Builder

  • From the UI: Open Quick Builder from the table’s context menu in view mode for immediate, guided exploration.
  • Via APIs: Use openQuickBuilder() and closeQuickBuilder() for programmatic control, enabling guided or customized analytic flows.

 

How Quick Builder evolves the navigation panel 

The navigation panel was originally introduced in analytic applications and can be opened programmatically using the script APIs. Quick Builder is an advanced evolution of the navigation panel, built on the new table experience. Compared with the navigation panel, it adds:
  • Deselect all
  • The compact display feature, previously available only at runtime, is now also supported during design time for BW Data Models
  • Separate arrangement of totals
  • Enabled by the story designer during design time
  • Launch from the context menu in addition to the existing script-based capability

It also intentionally changes some behaviours to streamline interaction:

  • No “available items” : To keep the interface clean and maximize canvas space, Quick Builder does not provide “available items” area.  All actions, such as adding or removing dimensions and measures, are available directly within Quick Builder.

 

Quick Builder vs. Data Analyzer

Use Quick Builder for lightweight, in‑story data exploration – ideally for fast table configuration that stay in context with your story. Use Data Analyzer for deeper, more comprehensive analysis. Results from Quick Builder apply seamlessly to story interactions such as linked analyses, filters, and other interactive features.

 

2026/QRC1: What to expect

  • New stories
    When you create a new story, the New Table Experience is applied by default. If you enable Quick Builder at design time, it will be available to viewers in these newly created stories.
  • Existing optimized stories
    If your story already contains a table, switch it to the New Table Experience and enable the “Enable Quick Builder” option in the table styling panel. Quick Builder will then be accessible to viewers at runtime through the context menu.
    If your story uses the Navigation Panel, the switch to the New Table Experience will seamlessly replace it with Quick Builder—no additional conversion or migration work required.
  • Converted classic stories and analytic applications
    When you convert a classic story or analytic application to an optimized story in QRC1, the New Table Experience is applied automatically. If you enable Quick Builder at design time, it will be available to viewers. If the classic analytic application uses the Navigation Panel, it is seamlessly replaced by Quick Builder—no manual migration needed.

 

2026/QRC2: What’s coming next

  • We plan to automatically convert existing tables to the New Table Experience during story design time wherever possible, removing the manual step described above. As part of this conversion, the Navigation Panel will be automatically replaced by Quick Builder—again, with no manual migration required.  

 

Getting started with the Quick Bilder in optimized stories:

  • For new stories, make sure Quick Builder is enabled during design time, or use the scripting API to open Quick Builder so that it is available at runtime.
  • For existing optimized stories, switch your tables to the New Table Experience and enable “Enable Quick Builder” in the table styling panel.
  • For classic stories and analytic applications you are converting—whether through manual conversion or mass migration—the resulting optimized stories will automatically use the New Table Experience starting with the QRC1 release. You only need to enable Quick Builder during design time, and it will be available to story viewers at runtime.

 

See it in Action:

 

 

Outlook

This release marks just the beginning of what Quick Builder will offer for light‑weight data exploration within stories. You can expect continuous enhancements - expanding Quick Builder to support additional widgets such as charts, and introducing new capabilities and functions, including filtering and more.

As always, your feedback plays a crucial role in shaping our roadmap. We’re excited to continue evolving together as we reimagine how Quick Builder can empower decision‑making and drive insights across the enterprise.

entry.title: "Protocol not allowed" error on PDF/Preview in (KBA 3617976) SAP Business One 10.0 FP2511 HANA
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

I am currently dealing with the "Protocol not allowed" error in SAP Business One 10.0 FP2511 (HANA) when users attempt to preview, print, or export documents to PDF immediately after using the Add & View mode.

The Issue: As documented in SAP KBA 3617976, the system blocks the print/preview protocol when a document is added or updated and then immediately accessed for output while still in the same view.

Current Situation: SAP has acknowledged this as a bug in FP2511 and intends to provide a fix in a future Feature Package. In the meantime, the suggested workarounds are:

  1. Using Add & New and navigating back to the record.

  2. Enabling Automatic Printing/PDF Export in Print Preferences.

The Challenge: The first workaround is significantly impacting user productivity in high-volume environments, and the second workaround (automatic PDF generation) isn't ideal for users who need to manually select layouts or review data before finalizing the output.

My Question to the Community: Aside from the two workarounds mentioned in the KBA, has anyone found a more seamless way to bypass this protocol error?

  • Is there a specific Client-side setting or UI API tweak that helps?

  • Has anyone found a way to "force" the protocol to refresh without navigating away from the document?

  • Are there any specific Windows Environment variables or Crystal Reports runtime settings that might alleviate this?

I would appreciate any creative solutions or "quick-fix" scripts that you've implemented for your clients while we wait for the official patch.

Best regards,

Sunil Patil

Certified SAP Business One Consultant

entry.title: Building a Powerful Succession Management Dashboard Using SAP SuccessFactors People Analytics
entry.content:
In today’s fast-changing business environment, succession planning is no longer a once-a-year HR activity — it’s a strategic necessity. With SAP SuccessFactors People Analytics, organizations can move from static succession spreadsheets to dynamic, real-time dashboards that provide deep visibility into talent risks and bench strength.

This blog walks you through a comprehensive Succession Management Dashboard built using People Analytics, covering high-level metrics and deep-dive KPIs.

Overview: Executive Snapshot Section

At the top of the dashboard, we present an executive summary using four key metrics:

Detailed KPIs Section

This section focuses on bench strength and succession risk categories.

  • Nomination Readiness Per Department

This KPI shows readiness distribution (R1, R2, R3) across departments.

  • R1 (Ready Now)
  • R2 (Ready in 1-2 Years)
  • R3 (Ready in 3-5 Years)

Business Value:

  • Identifies strong vs weak talent pipelines.
  • Enables targeted development programs.
  • Supports workforce planning discussions.
  • Critical Positions Per Department

Displays count of critical roles grouped by department.

Why it matters:

  • Highlights departments with higher operational risk.
  • Supports prioritization of succession planning efforts.

Succession Risk Categorization

This is the most powerful section of the dashboard — identifying succession gaps.

Without Incumbent and/or R1 Readiness Successors

Definition:

  • Positions are vacant

OR

  • No “Ready Now” successor exists

Risk Level: High

These positions need immediate attention — recruitment or accelerated development.

With Incumbent but Without R1 Readiness Successors

Definition:

  • Role has an incumbent
  • No Ready Now successor

Risk Level: Medium-High

This indicates dependency on a single individual.

Incumbent & R1 Available but No R2 Successors

Definition:

  • Immediate backup exists
  • No mid-term pipeline

Risk Level: Medium

Short-term risk covered, but long-term succession weak.

Incumbent, R1 & R2 Available

Definition:

  • Strong short-term and mid-term pipeline

Risk Level: Low

Represents succession maturity.

Successor Readiness Per Department

This report provides:

  • Total successors nominated
  • Readiness distribution per department
  • Average successors per critical position
  • Positions with zero successors

Insight Example:

If Finance has 15 critical positions but only 2 R1 successors, it signals urgent development planning.

How It’s Built in SAP SuccessFactors People Analytics

Using People Analytics Story Reports:

  • Data Source: Succession Domain + Position Management
  • Join Position, Incumbent, and Successor entities
  • Create calculated columns:
    • Readiness Count (R1, R2)
    • Succession Risk Category
    • Coverage Ratio
  • Use visualizations:
    • KPI Tiles
    • Stacked Column Charts
    • Heatmaps
    • Department Drill-down Tables

Role-Based Permissions (RBP) ensure leaders see only their departments.

Business Impact of This Dashboard

  • Improves leadership confidence
  • Reduces single-point-of-failure risk
  • Supports board-level talent reviews
  • Enables proactive workforce planning
  • Moves HR from operational reporting to strategic advisory

Final Thoughts

A Succession Management Dashboard in SAP SuccessFactors People Analytics transforms talent planning from reactive to predictive.

Instead of asking:

“Do we have successors?”

You start asking:

“Are we succession-ready as an organization?”

And that’s where HR truly becomes strategic.

entry.title: Decision Logic Modernization in SAP Integrations: Using RPT-1 for Intelligent Routing Decisions
entry.content:
Integration modernization is often discussed in two dimensions:

  1. Platform modernization – moving from older stacks like SAP NetWeaver PI/PO, SAP Neo, or legacy 3rd party platforms to SAP Integration Suite.
  2. Scenario modernization – shifting from legacy protocols to modern integration patterns such as APIs & event-driven architectures; upgrading ABAP / Java mapping to graphical mapping / groovy script; or moving from basic auth to OAuth 2.0 or client certificate based authentication, and so on.

But there is another Modernization opportunity at the scenario level: decision logic modernization.

In this blog, we explore how routing decision logic, as an orchestration step — can be modernized using RPT-1, SAP’s latest foundation model hosted on BTP AI Core (Generative AI Hub) — applied to an outbound delivery routing scenario.

The Traditional Approach: Static Rules, Lookups, and Growing Complexity

In SAP PI/PO and Integration Suite, routing decisions are typically implemented using deterministic, rule-based logic. These decisions often control how a scenario behaves – including which receiver system to call, or which transformation branch to follow. The common approaches include:

  • Static routing via Routers based on payload elements or fields
  • Dynamic configurations, such as externalized parameters passed at runtime
  • Lookups to external systems (e.g., via JDBC, SOAP, RFC lookups) to evaluate conditions at runtime — achieved in Integration Suite via patterns like Request-Reply or Content Enricher

These mechanisms work — but they come with growing challenges:

  • Rules get combinatorially complex with more conditions and evolving business scenarios
  • Lookup tables become burdensome to maintain
  • Decisions become rigid and hard to evolve over time

As a result, rule-based decisioning becomes harder to maintain as business requirements evolve. Agility suffers. Adaptation becomes expensive.

What if, instead of relying on static rule-based logic, iflows could make decisions based on historical business patterns — applying predictive machine learning without the overhead of training and managing custom models? With the in-context learning capabilities of SAP’s latest foundation model, RPT-1, such predictions can be performed on-the-fly at runtime.


Enter RPT-1: Foundation Model for Predictive Tasks

SAP’s RPT-1 is a pre-trained foundation model available via BTP AI Core (Generative AI Hub). Unlike traditional ML services that require dedicated data science setup, RPT-1 offers:

  • Pre-trained model, ready for “plug and play” in your scenario
  • No ML infrastructure setup
  • No data science expertise required
  • No-code consumption via API
  • Faster time-to-market
  • Democratization of AI capabilities across IT functions

SAP RPT-1 is pre-trained specifically for tasks such as classification and prediction over ERP tabular data, unlike many general-purpose GenAI models that are primarily trained on large corpora of natural language text. Instead of building and managing a full ML lifecycle, integration developers can now leverage predictive intelligence directly into iflows — without any data science related heavy-lifting.

If you are new to SAP RPT-1, I recommend that you read this introductory blog.
Also, explore this SAP-RPT-1 playground.

Business Scenario: Intelligent Outbound Delivery Routing with RPT-1
SmartSense Technologies (SST), a global provider of smart security devices, faces challenges in routing outbound deliveries from its manufacturing plants to distribution centers, retail stores, and end customers. Their current routing logic, embedded in integration flows, relies on static rules and lookup tables – employing a combination of Material, Quantity, Delivery priority, Material group, Ship to location, and so on. This leads to frequent shipping delays, cost overruns, and SLA violations whenever logistics conditions change and sub-optimal 3PL partner is chosen.

To overcome these limitations, SST decides to leverage SAP Integration Suite together with SAP RPT-1 foundation model. By embedding machine learning–driven decision-making into its integration flow, SST aims to dynamically select the optimal logistics partner for each delivery based on historical performance, cost, and delivery times. While RPT-1 is capable of solving predictive tasks such as classification and regression, we will be using it for classification task — i.e. to classify given delivery (defined by delivery header & item attributes) to most optimal 3PL logistics provider.

The following diagram illustrates the target architecture implemented by SST for intelligent outbound delivery routing using SAP Integration Suite and RPT-1 model, hosted on BTP’s Generative AI hub:


Step 1: Outbound Delivery Creation
An outbound delivery is created in SAP S/4HANA, which triggers an outbound notification event. The event is consumed by the iflow, which then calls S/4HANA OData API to fetch the following delivery related header and item attributes.

Delivery header fields: DeliveryDate, SalesOrganization, ShipToParty, SoldToParty, DeliveryDocumentType, CreationDate, ShippingPoint, DeliveryPriority, IncotermsClassification, TransactionCurrency

Delivery item fields: Material, DeliveryDocumentItemText, MaterialGroup, ActualDeliveryQuantity, DeliveryQuantityUnit, Plant, StorageLocation, ItemGrossWeight, ItemWeightUnit, ControllingArea, DistributionChannel, GoodsMovementType, ProfitCenter

 

Step 2: Context Selection – Random Historical Records
We store historic labeled data i.e. delivery headers, items and corresponding optimal 3PL partner data in SAP HANA Cloud. The iflow is configured to fetch a random set of this historic data from HANA Cloud. As recommended in this blog, for most enterprise use-cases, where you have about hundred thousand to million lines of labelled data, depending on how diverse your scenario data is, you may choose the context size, by iterating and finalizing the apt number of historical records to pick. By picking random records, we are able to provide statistically diverse examples, improve prediction quality and avoid bias from sequential records.

The Iflow executes the following query on the HANA Cloud DB:

SELECT TOP ${property.NumberofRecords}
"DeliveryDate",   "DeliveryDocumentNumber",  "CreationDate", "DeliveryPriority",  "IncotermsClassification",  "Material", "DeliveryDocumentItemText", "MaterialGroup",  "ActualDeliveryQuantity", "DeliveryQuantityUnit", "ItemGrossWeight", "ItemWeightUnit", "DistributionChannel", "PartnerNumber"
FROM "RPT1"."historic_3pl_selections"
ORDER BY RAND()


Step 3: Constructing the RPT-1 Request

We now combine:

  • Historical random records (known 3PL partner)
  • The current outbound delivery (3PL partner to be predicted)

The current transaction includes a placeholder: [PREDICT]

The request payload contains:

  • 900 historical labeled rows
  • 1 new outbound delivery row marked for prediction

For detailed info on payload construction, refer to the official documentation page and the GitHub repository (sample Postman/Bruno collections available for experimentation).

Further, while creating the inferencing request to RPT-1, we trim the columns from original 24 down to only 14 columns of delivery attributes, by removing fields like Sales Org, Controlling Area, Profit centre that do not influence partner selection. This is one of the best practices to achieve best possible predictive outputs. Read more recommended best practices here.
 

Step 4: Deploying RPT-1 in Generative AI Hub


Step 5: RPT-1 Response

The response contains Number of predicted records, predicted field (here optimal 3PL partner) along with Confidence score.

Response from our example scenario:

You can also send multiple unknown records in one batch. To read more about the number of max. records and columns supported by RPT-1, check out this help page.

Step 6: Intelligent Routing in the Iflow

The Iflow now:

  1. Reads the predicted PartnerNumber
  2. Evaluates confidence threshold (optional safeguard)
  3. Routes outbound delivery to the optimal 3PL partner
  4. Optionally, logs decision + confidence for traceability in HANA Cloud.


When AI in Iflows Brings Real Business Value

  • When routing decisions directly impact business outcomes
    If sub-optimal routing can lead to SLA breaches, shipment delays, higher costs, or customer dissatisfaction, intelligent decision logic creates measurable advantage.
  • When rule-based logic becomes complex and brittle
    As business variables multiply (products, regions, priorities, partners), static rules become hard to maintain and scale. ML handles multi-dimensional patterns more effectively.
  • When historical ERP data contains predictive patterns
    If past delivery and fulfillment data reflects consistent business behavior, ML can learn from it and improve future routing decisions beyond deterministic conditions.
  • When business context evolves over time
    With changing product mixes, customer bases, or distribution models, retrainable ML models adapt dynamically — avoiding constant manual rule maintenance.

Not every integration scenario requires ML capabilities. But where routing decisions carry operational or financial impact, replacing rigid rule sets with adaptive decision logic can significantly improve resilience and maintainability. Decision logic modernization is therefore not about adding AI everywhere — it is about applying it where it meaningfully improves orchestration.

Note: This blog revisits our earlier implementation in light of the planned deprecation of SAP BTP Data Attribute Recommendation, demonstrating an updated approach using SAP-RPT-1 model.

entry.title: Re: CPQ: How to Add a second tab in product configuration...
entry.content:
Hi @flachlaender 

Please check the Tab Visibility settings under:

Product Definition ->Layout ->Add/Edit Tab (click on top left corner)

In most cases, the GENERAL tab is set to "Display tab only if it is not empty."

 

 

If no attributes are assigned to the GENERAL tab, or if those attributes are hidden by visibility rules/permissions, CPQ will not render the GENERAL tab, and the new tab will appear instead of next to GENERAL.

So please verify:

  1. That the GENERAL tab contains at least one visible attribute

  2. That those attributes are not hidden by visibility rules

  3. That there are no role/permission restrictions affecting the tab or its attributes

If needed, temporarily switch GENERAL to "Always displayed" to confirm whether this is a visibility issue.

entry.title: Re: SAP TM Report /SCMTMS/CONDITION_CC- Check and Repair consistency of conditions
entry.content:
Hi , Rogiero , thanks for sharing sch new function. 

would you please check support on the following issue during using thee BFR+ 

We are using capacity doc. creation with the condition /SCMTMS/TOR_TYPE in the planning profile to determine the Freight Order (FO) type based on Freight Unit (FU) attributes (e.g., FU Type, DG, Origin Country).

Observed behavior in manual and automatic assign/ assign selected items in cockpit:

  • When planning a single FU the FO type determination works correctly.
  • When planning multiple FUs with different FU types, the system combines them into one FO and applies one FO type (typically from the first FU).
    ➜ This is wrong for our process; each FU type should lead to its own FO type.

Tried already

  • Verified /SCMTMS/TOR_TYPE condition in planning profile.
  • Tested with VSR and manual planning.
  • Maintained FU–FU incompatibility (split by FU Type / DG).
    → System shows a warning, but still allows saving one FO (no auto-split, no separate FO types).

Requirement

  • When selecting multiple FUs with different FU types, the system must automatically create separate FOs, each with the correct FO type according to /SCMTMS/TOR_TYPE.

entry.title: Re: OPA5 vs wdi5 vs Playwright-SAP: pros and cons?
entry.content:
Dear @theodore_abitbol,

Your comparison is largely correct but I would add 4 more points:

1. wdi5 is actually referenced as a recommended tool in the official SAPUI5 testing guidance. In SAPUI5 Testing Pyramid documentation, SAP lists OPA5 and wdi5 under Recommended Tools. SAP explicitly explains that wdi5 provides UI5 control locators + UI5 synchronization on top of WebdriverIO, running against a real browser. This is a key nuance for audit/certification narratives wdi5 is not built into UI5 like OPA5 but it is referenced positively in SAPUI5 official testing guidance

2. SAP explicitly recommends OPA5 for integration testing and it is part of SAPUI5/QUnit. SAPUI5 states We recommend OPA5 for integration tests and describes OPA5 as part of SAPUI5, built on top of QUnit also SAPUI5 describes OPA5 as part of SAPUI5, built on top of QUnit

3. UIVeri5 is deprecated by SAP, and SAP points to WDIO + wdi5 as an alternative
SAP official GitHub repo for UIVeri5 states it is deprecated and suggests considering WDIO + WDI5 as an alternative. This is another strong SAP-facing argument that wdi5 is an accepted direction

4. Playwright / Playwright-SAP are not part of SAPUI5 official recommended tools guidance
In the same SAPUI5 Testing Pyramid page, SAP recommends OPA5 and wdi5 Playwright is not listed there as a recommended tool so if certification/audit defensibility is a primary goal, Playwright-SAP can be great technically but it’s harder to justify as SAP-recommended practice based strictly on official SAPUI5 documentation

Summary:

  • Keep OPA5/QUnit as the most defensible SAP-recommended backbone for integration tests
  • Consider wdi5 for broader end-to-end coverage with UI5 control-level stability because it is also referenced as recommended in SAPUI5 testing guidance and aligns with SAP own direction away from UIVeri5
  • Use Playwright(-SAP) only if you accept that it is outside what SAPUI5 currently lists as recommended tooling from an official guidance standpoint

More details below:

https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/sdk/docs/topics/7cdee404cac441888539ed7bfe076e57.html

https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/sdk            

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAPUI5/538009aec85e4e99b31f4d2de2443abe/dac59fadd5f9419d986f74ba602c6d29.html

https://github.com/SAP/ui5-uiveri5

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Unearthing Efficiencies in Acquire to Decommission process with S/4HANA and Signavio Process Mining
entry.content:
Acquire to Decommission Process in S/4HANA

The Acquire to Decommission value chain in SAP S/4HANA manages the entire lifecycle of an enterprise asset, from its initial capital request to its final disposal. It integrates financial accounting with physical maintenance to ensure that every asset is utilized at peak performance for the lowest possible cost. By centralizing this data, S/4HANA provides a "single source of truth" that connects procurement, fixed asset accounting, and plant maintenance into one fluid stream.

 SAP Signavio Plug and Gain

The SAP Signavio Plug and Gain model allows legacy ERP users to jumpstart process mining by using Generative AI to automatically extract and map "as-is" processes, with instant data. Organizations first use Process Insights for a rapid health check and Gen AI-driven correction tips, then move to Process Intelligence for deep-dive simulations. This adoption path identifies hidden inefficiencies and outdated, inactive data, enabling a Fit-to-Standard approach. By analyzing existing data and process before migrating, enterprises ensure their move to S/4HANA Asset Management is fast, low-risk, and focused on high-value operations.

In this blog, we will discuss an example,  how SAP Signavio Process Intelligence and SAP Signavio Process insights scans the maintenance order process towards transformation.

Transformation via Process Discovery

The Process Discovery widget automatically constructs a visual representation of how maintenance, supply chain, and finance interact in real time. It maps out these hidden rework scenarios by showing "feedback loops" in your process flow. In the given sample of Maintenance Order process, Instead of a smooth forward motion It highlights the real-world complexity of your process like Confirmation cancellation, TECO cancellation, Material return and reissue. This creates a "spaghetti" effect where the lines tangle around specific activities. By analyzing the cycle time of these loops, you can see exactly how much time is wasted re-doing tasks, helping you identify which equipment or work centers struggle with "First-Time-Right" execution.

 

 

Strategic Impact of the Variant Explorer

The Variant Explorer widget groups your data into specific patterns to show the most common routes maintenance orders take. It allows you to contrast the "happy path" with deviant behaviors, such as variants that involve multiple cancellations or repeated technical completions. By clicking through these variants, you can immediately see the frequency and impact of these disruptions; for instance, you might find that while the standard process takes few days, the rework variant with a TECO cancellation spikes the cycle time. This widget simplifies complex data into clear categories, making it easy to see which process deviations are driving up your labor costs and extending asset downtime.

 

In addition, by integrating attributes such as Equipment, Priority, Work Center, Asset criticality and Cost into SAP Signavio Process Intelligence, we can perform a highly detailed analysis that goes beyond simple process mapping to uncover deep operational insights. Refer the below blog for a detailed attribute analysis on Asset Management.

SAP Signavio Process Insights

SAP Signavio Process Insights provides a direct look at how your Acquire to Decommission value chain is actually performing by connecting straight to your SAP S/4HANA data. The system utilizes inbuilt process flows—specifically Asset Master creation to deactivation, Maintenance notification management, and Maintenance order management—to automatically map the lifecycle of your equipment without manual modeling. It identifies common problems that slow down these flows, such as overdue maintenance orders, pending TECO completions, and unreleased orders that have already passed their start dates. By identifying these specific blockers, the tool shows you exactly where the work is piling up and why your assets might be sitting idle instead of running.

The real value lies in the system’s Industry Benchmarking, which compares your performance indicators—like Order Type usage, pending notifications, and Asset Class usage per company code—against peer companies. If your data shows a high volume of pending notifications or inconsistent order types across different regions, the system flags these as gaps. Through Guided Analysis, it provides Correction Recommendations to fix current backlogs and Innovation Recommendations to improve long-term asset health. This benchmarks your efficiency against the top of your industry, helping you optimize every stage of the asset lifecycle from procurement to retirement.

 

The combination of SAP Signavio and S/4HANA Asset Management rejuvenates the Acquire to Decommission value chain by replacing legacy complexity with data-driven, standardized processes. This transformation moves your organization from reactive maintenance to a high-performance culture focused on predictive excellence and maximum asset uptime.

For more details, pls refer,

https://bestruncompany.us10.process-insights.cloud.sap/reference-guide/b04ffa9cf42145169083d9d3b2496a04.html

https://www.signavio.com/highlights/plug-and-gain-approach/

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/how-to-improve-your-acquire-to-decommission-process-using-plug-and-gain-by/ba-p/13625924#:~:text=The%20data%2C%20as%20customary%20with,or%20expertise%20in%20process%20mining

entry.title: How to handle multiple cost categories per PO line in the GR/IR Reconciliation Fiori App?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I would like to ask for advice regarding the Reconcile GR/IR Accounts Fiori App in SAP S/4HANA.

In our setup, it is common that a single Purchase Order line contains multiple distinct cost categories, such as:

  • Material
  • Freight
  • Customs etc.

Many purchasing organizations are using POs where imports automatically generate several cost components bundled into the same PO item. Although they originate from the same PO, these categories follow different internal rules, for example:

  • separate aging logic
  • different responsible departments
  • different analysis and resolution workflows

Because of these differences, each category needs to be analyzed and processed as its own entity.

Is there any configuration, enhancement option, or best practice that allows the GR/IR Reconciliation App to treat each cost category within a PO line as a distinct analytical item?

In other words:
Is it possible to split or represent multiple cost categories from the same PO line separately within the app, so that they can follow their respective workflow, aging logic, and responsible teams?

Any insights or experiences would be very welcome.

Thank you!

entry.title: Make the Most of Your Time at SAP Sapphire
entry.content:
Attending a major event like SAP Sapphire is both exciting and overwhelming. From the moment you arrive, you’re surrounded by energy—thousands of conversations happening at once, dozens of sessions competing for your attention, and new ideas around every corner. Opportunity is everywhere, but time is limited.

At Sapphire, time is your most valuable asset. With the right approach, every hour can deliver insight, inspiration, and connections that last well beyond the event itself. Sapphire is not just something you attend; it’s an experience you can actively shape.

Many people still think of conferences as passive experiences: a series of presentations in large rooms, a few notes taken, and a flight home. Sapphire is different. It is designed as an immersive environment filled with ideas, people, information, and experiences. All designed around you— to help you address your challenges and achieve your goals.

People come to Sapphire for many reasons: to hear the latest announcements, explore emerging technologies, deepen their expertise, or build meaningful professional relationships. Whatever brings you here, success starts with intention. This overview of the Sapphire content program is designed to help you plan ahead, navigate the event with confidence, and make the most of every minute you spend onsite.

 

Keynotes: Get the Big Picture

The Sapphire keynotes are where everything begins. These sessions provide the strategic foundation for the entire event, offering a clear, high‑level view of SAP’s vision, priorities, and direction.

In the keynotes, you’ll hear major announcements, product innovations, and insights into how SAP is responding to global business challenges. These sessions connect market trends, customer needs, and technology strategy into a cohesive story that helps you understand not just what is changing, but why it matters.

Keynotes are also designed to inspire. They give you the opportunity to step away from day‑to‑day execution and think bigger—about transformation, growth, and what’s possible when technology is used with purpose. For many attendees, the keynotes set the tone for the entire event, providing context that makes everything else more meaningful.

Attending the keynotes helps you make better decisions throughout Sapphire. The themes and priorities introduced on the keynote stage reappear in demos, theater sessions, and customer conversations, giving you a consistent lens through which to evaluate what you see.

 

The Experience Center: Where Innovation Comes to Life

If the keynotes define the vision, the Experience Center is where that vision becomes real. This immersive space brings the SAP solution portfolio to life through engaging, end‑to‑end demonstrations that show how technologies work together to solve real business problems.

The Experience Center is designed for exploration. Rather than focusing on individual features in isolation, it highlights complete scenarios—how data flows across systems, how insights are generated, and how intelligent applications support better decision‑making across the enterprise.

This is where abstract concepts turn into tangible outcomes. Seeing solutions in action in this way provides clarity that is difficult to achieve through presentations alone. Many attendees find that a single walkthrough in the Experience Center can unlock new ideas or spark conversations they continue throughout the event.

To get the most value, plan time here intentionally. Focus on the areas most relevant to your role, industry, or current challenges. Ask questions, engage with the presenters, and think about how what you’re seeing could apply within your own organization. The Experience Center is designed to inspire—and those moments of inspiration are often what attendees remember most.

 

Theater Sessions: Learn at Your Own Pace, on Your Terms

One of the greatest strengths of SAP Sapphire is the breadth and flexibility of its theater sessions. These are designed to meet you where you are, allowing you to build a personalized learning journey that fits your goals and level of expertise.

Overview sessions provide a high‑level introduction to topics and solutions, helping you quickly establish context. Strategy sessions focus on how to achieve specific business objectives, outlining approaches, best practices, and lessons learned.

Customer Success Stories offer a practical perspective, featuring SAP customers sharing real‑world experiences. These sessions highlight challenges, decisions, and results, providing insight into what transformation looks like in practice.

Solution demos and deep dives offer a closer look at capabilities and workflows, while road map reviews help you understand what’s coming next. “Ask the Expert” sessions provide an opportunity to get detailed answers to specific questions.

 

Demo Stations and Expert Stations: One‑on‑One, High‑Value Engagement

Some of the most impactful moments at Sapphire happen outside traditional sessions. Demo stations and expert stations are designed to support focused, one‑on‑one engagement with SAP specialists.

At demo stations, you can explore specific products and features in a conversational, informal setting. Expert stations allow you to consult directly with SAP professionals about complex or unique challenges.

Preparation makes these interactions even more valuable. Come with a clear understanding of your challenges, constraints, and goals, and you’ll leave with insights that are immediately actionable.

 

Sponsor Booths and Sessions: Expand Your Perspective

SAP Sapphire also offers the opportunity to engage with a broad ecosystem of partners who extend and complement SAP solutions. Sponsor booths and sessions showcase technologies and services that address specialized needs or bring innovative approaches to common business challenges.

Approach these interactions with curiosity and focus. Look for partners aligned with your priorities and explore how their offerings fit into your broader strategy.

 

Missed a Session? Continue the Experience Online

It’s impossible to see everything Sapphire has to offer. Many key sessions are recorded and made available on the Sapphire virtual platform after the event, allowing you to revisit content and share insights with colleagues.

Think of the virtual platform as an extension of Sapphire—giving you space to reflect, prioritize, and learn even more about the topics that inspired you at the in-person event.

 

Start Planning Now

The most successful Sapphire experiences are intentional. Define what success looks like for you, plan accordingly, and use Sapphire as a catalyst for progress.

To start planning your experience, visit: https://www.sap.com/events/sapphire.html

entry.title: Project Budget Report - Budget (AVC) Value
entry.content:
Hi!

We are building a Project Budget Report Custom Reporting App in Public Cloud.

Although there is a standard app which provides all the reporting details, but it is applicable only for services and not for materials.
To provide reporting details of materials + services, we have approached our client to build a custom app.

Currently the challenge we are facing is fetching the Budget (AVC) value.
I have checked CDS views related to WBS elements, but could not find any appropriate CDS where the Total budget for a WBS Element can be fetched.

As per the process, a project is created, a WBS is assigned under it and initial budget is uploaded.

The fields we are displaying in output is similar the standard app.

1. Actuals
2. Commitments
3. Withheld Commitments (displays values if document is under approval)
4. Available Budget
5. Total Budget

We have already implemented the logic to display the values in the 1st three fields based on the values of the documents posted (PR - PO - GR) and it is working fine

If we can fetch this total budget value, then subsequently we can apply the calculations to derive the Available Budget = (Total Budget - (Commitments+Witheld Commitments+Actuals) for a WBS Element. That way our report can be completed.

Can anyone help me the resolve this. We are at 80% completion for this development.

 

Thanks

You need to have a Pay-As-You-Go contract for your global account. If your account is purely subscription-based it will not be possible to use this service, as it is exclusively available under the PAYG model. entry.title: What is behind R3TR XPRA, for instance RV80HGEN
entry.content:
What happens when a R3TR XPRA entry of a workbench transport request is imported?

For instance, with R3TR XPRA RV80HGEN. When this entry is imported, "all formulas indicated as being "active" in TFRM are activated for the application programs" (formulas/routines defined in the transaction VOFM), as explained by the note 22808 - Transferring formulas - SAP for Me).

Thanks.

Sandra

entry.title: Re: What is behind R3TR XPRA, for instance RV80HGEN
entry.content:
The XPRA program is executed during the phase "Method Execution" of the import (see the transport request log):

The phase "Method Execution" starts the background job RDDEXECL, user DDIC, in the client 000 of the target system, and the job has one step starting the program RDDEXECL.

For information, the phase "Method Execution" is also used to start the "before export" and "after import" function modules which can be configured via the transaction code SOBJ. They are called "methods" in SOBJ. You may create your own function modules if you need to add special logic when transporting. Standard examples are:

  • roles (SOBJ object ACGR, which has the "methods"/function modules PRGN_AFTER_IMP_ACTGROUP_ACGR and PRGN_BEFORE_EXP_ACTGROUP_ACGR),
  • flexible workflow (SOBJ object WSSC),
  • Custom Field Logic (SOBJ objects CFDF and more), etc.
  • NB: I don't know how Adaptation Transport Organizer (ATO) is used for transporting parts of flexible workflow, CFL (table ATO_PK_ITEMS/Fiori App F1589 Register Extensions for Transport).

Here's the call chain of the program RDDEXECL: submit of program RDDEXECU > function module TRINT_CALL_XPRA of function group SCTS_EXE_EXP > subroutine EXECUTE_XPRA > call the XPRA program :

 

SUBMIT (ps_xpra-program) AND RETURN
      TO SAP-SPOOL WITHOUT SPOOL DYNPRO
      SPOOL PARAMETERS ss_pri_params.

 

Here is the job and the call stack with RV80HGEN (impossible to show here that RV80HGEN was submitted by the subroutine EXECUTE_XPRA, because it's via SUBMIT):

Note that RDDEXECU may also call TRINT_CALL_XCLA to execute the entries R3TR XCLA <class>. XCLA classes must inherit from IF_CTS_TABLE_CONVERSION, they are much more specialized (table conversions) than XPRA programs. The main logic is defined in the method IF_XCLA_EXECUTOR~RUN of the class CL_XCLA_EXECUTOR.

As the program RDDEXECL always runs in the client 000, an XPRA program can be used to do very general tasks, often on workbench objects, table conversion, etc.
The XPRA program doesn't have any parameter. Impossible to know in which client the transport request is imported.
It calls the function modules TR_APPEND_LOG and TR_FLUSH_LOG to display messages in the transport log.
The XPRA program can run (i.e. the transport request is imported) several times in case there are errors or whatever reason.

The log of the transport request contains the result of the XPRA program.
Here, for RV80HGEN, there's only one message: "All routines have been successfully activated" (VC556):

PS: if you want an XPRA RV80HGEN entry being added automatically to a transport request when you add a formula/routine in VOFM, you may apply the instructions of the note 598475 - XPRA RV80HGEN during transport of VOFM objects - SAP for Me. SAP say that it's a modification of the system, so you'll have to handle this as a modification each time you upgrade your software (SPAU).

entry.title: Re: CPQ: When is the Script in the Scripting tab in the p...
entry.content:
Hi @flachlaender 

There are two ways to implement product-level scripting:

  1. Add a script directly in the Product-> Scripts tab and attach it to one or more product-related events listed below (e.g. When product is loaded, When product rule execution starts, When product is ready, etc.).

  2. Create a Global Script and attach it to the relevant product events.

The script executes when the selected event is triggered.

The difference is scope:
A script defined directly in the product runs only for that product.
A global script can be reused across multiple products by attaching it to the required events.

You can find the list and behaviour of product-related events in the official documentation:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CPQ/7fc27540a0f14bd1a10b639117a9d4d7/2c2afa74b1444c16a5b914d5c60ab9cc.html?locale=en-US

Yes, this is IronPython.

Within product scope, attributes can be accessed using:

Product.Attributes.GetByName("Attr1")
or the shorter form:
Product.Attr("Attr1")

The choice between product-specific and global scripting depends on whether the logic should remain isolated or reusable.

entry.title: Re: CPQ: How to Add a second tab in product configuration...
entry.content:
Ah, I see your point now.

The behaviour you are observing is standard.

The GENERAL tab is treated as the default configuration container when only a single tab is present. In that scenario, the tab header is not rendered, and the configuration is displayed directly.

However, once you add additional tabs, CPQ expects an explicit tab structure. The built-in GENERAL tab does not behave as a normal, fully customisable tab in multi-tab layouts.

If you intend to use multiple tabs consistently, the recommended approach is to create custom tab(s) and explicitly assign attributes to them, rather than relying on the default GENERAL tab.

In short:
GENERAL works as a single-tab container, but for multi-tab configurations, it is better to define and manage your own tabs explicitly.

entry.title: Re: CPQ: How do I check the value of a hidden attribute?
entry.content:
Hi @flachlaender, 

A Hidden, Calculated, no Matching attribute derives its value from the formula defined in the attribute configuration from the Hint field).. It is calculated before product rules are executed and is intended to compute values based on other attributes, not to be dynamically assigned by rules.

If you plan to use scripts, you should keep this execution order in mind.

If the value needs to be controlled via product rule or triggers  it is better to change the display type to Free Input,set  no Matchin,g and remove the attribute from the layout. This keeps it non-visible in the configurator while allowing rules or triggers to assign and override its value reliably using:

<*AssignValue:YourAttributeName:ValueYouWant*>

To verify the value during testing, use the Formula Validator and reference the attribute as:

<*VALUE(YourAttributeName)*>

This allows you to inspect the calculated or assigned value without exposing the attribute in the UI.

entry.title: Introducing the new decoupled JavaScript hosting service for SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
SAP is excited to introduce our next-generation JavaScript hosting service designed to power high-performance, cloud-native storefronts on SAP Commerce Cloud. Delivered through SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), the new JavaScript hosting service offers flexibility, scalability, and developer-friendly tooling needed for today’s headless and composable commerce experiences.
The release is planned for June 2026.

About the new JavaScript hosting service

The new JavaScript hosting service is purpose-built to give front-end teams a fast, reliable, and modern environment for deploying and operating commerce storefront applications. Key capabilities include:

  • Hosting for composable storefront, with modern architecture and improvements over the current Commerce Cloud JavaScript hosting service
  • Server Side Rendering (SSR) for SEO performance and faster first load experiences
  • Integrated Content Delivery Network (CDN) for secure, performant, globally optimized delivery of storefront assets
  • Automated build and deployment workflows, initiated by code changes or environment variable updates
  • Support for multiple projects, ideal for multi-brand or multi-region businesses
  • Deployment variables for seamless environment configuration across builds
  • Integrated Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) to simplify development of non-UI logic and improve performance and security
Why move to the new JavaScript hosting service?

As the new JavaScript hosting service evolves, it is positioned to become the strategic hosting solution for SAP Commerce Cloud frontends. Benefits include:

  • Decoupled JavaScript builds, so that the front‑end builds run independently of backend builds, unlike the current Commerce Cloud JavaScript hosting service
  • Deployment variables, for clean, automated environment configuration
  • A modern BTP-based architecture that unlocks greater scalability, operational stability, and future extensibility
  • Cloud‑native best practices such as automated builds, configuration-as-data, and predictable deployments

The new JavaScript hosting service helps organizations modernize their storefront delivery pipelines, reduce operational overhead, and accelerate time to market.

CDN integration and future CDN flexibility

The new JavaScript hosting service includes a built‑in CDN to ensure fast, globally distributed content delivery. In the future, customers will also have the option to connect their own CDN, should they need tighter control or want to integrate with an existing enterprise CDN strategy.

New Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) server

The included new Backend-for-Frontend server (or BFF) is an optional but powerful middleware server that centralizes non‑UI logic, improves performance, and streamlines extensibility. Benefits of using a BFF include:

  • Smaller and cleaner front‑end codebases focused on user experience
  • Optimized backend API aggregation to reduce network calls and improve SEO and performance
  • Easier integration of third-party data sources for composable architectures
  • Reduced exposure of sensitive data, improving security
  • Parallel development between front‑end teams and BFF logic builders, enabling faster iteration cycles

Usage of the BFF for composable storefront is optional. The current release of composable storefront does not currently use the BFF and there are no plans to add support in 2026. The BFF provides a strong foundation for future-ready, modular architectures, and customers and partners are encouraged to take advantage of the BFF for their customizations or custom applications. 

Does the decoupled JavaScript hosting service replace the current JavaScript hosting service?

The BTP-based decoupled JavaScript hosting service does not immediately replace the current JavaScript hosting service. The current JavaScript hosting service remains available. The intent is for all customers to move to the new JavaScript hosting service in the future. SAP will announce deprecation timelines at a later date. Customers starting new projects are strongly encouraged to use the new JavaScript hosting service from the start.

Next Steps

If you’re building on Commerce Cloud and want a modern, scalable approach to hosting your storefront, the new BTP-based JavaScript hosting service is ready to support your journey. Migration guidance will be shared as the rollout progresses.

To join the pilot or early adopter program, please contact your account representative or email me directly. We’d be happy to discuss the details with you!

entry.title: Re: CPQ: How to get and set attribute value in global scr...
entry.content:
Hi @flachlaender 

The issue is related to the execution context.

Your script is attached to a quote-level event (e.g. after updating products in the quote). In that context, you are working with a quote item, not directly with the product definition object. This is why Product is None, resulting in the NoneType error.

If you are attaching a Global script to the Product, you need to deselect all of the quote-level events you added, save the script (without attaching it to any event here), then go to Product -> Scripts  ->Click on Add Script -> Global Script, find your script, and select one or more of the listed product events.

In short, the problem is not with the attribute itself, but with the context in which the global script is executed.

entry.title: Re: Issue with Employee Status Picklist in Event reason FO
entry.content:
This is due to your Profile Language settings you have set in your instance. If you have English US and English GB available and only maintained English_GB in the label, make sure that your langugage settings of your profile (Profile>Settings) is set to English_GB language too, else you will see blank data. 

Other task you need to do is maintain all translation of the label so you won't have to change your langugae settings

entry.title: Re: Unable to create ABAP trail instance in BTP trail acc...
entry.content:
Hi Lokesh,

In the trial landscape, you can have only one ABAP environment instance per global account. This error typically occurs if:

  1. An existing ABAP instance is already created (even if partially failed).
  2. Entitlements are not correctly assigned to the subaccount.
  3. The previous instance is stuck in 'Creation failed' state.

In trial account, quota limitations are strict, and sometimes cleanup is required before creating a new instance.

Regards,

Meenakshi.

entry.title: Re: Career Advice: BRIM vs IS-Utilities for an ABAP Consultant
entry.content:
Hi there,

Great question — and it’s good that you’re thinking about your long‑term direction this early in your career.

Since you already have 3 years of ABAP experience in SAP CRM, both BRIM and IS‑Utilities are realistic paths, but they lead to very different types of work.

If you’re looking for faster market demand and broader opportunities: 👉 IS‑Utilities (IS‑U) is the safer and more stable choice. Utilities companies in North America and Europe are actively modernizing, moving to S/4HANA, and adopting BTP extensions. IS‑U functional consultants are in high demand, and your ABAP background will help you understand billing, device management, and FI‑CA processes quickly.

If you’re interested in high‑value niche consulting: 👉 BRIM is excellent, but it’s more specialized. BRIM projects are usually in telecom, media, subscription‑based industries, and large enterprises. The learning curve is steeper (CI, FI‑CA, SOM, CC), but the roles are well‑paid and often more architecture‑focused.

A simple way to decide:

  • If you enjoy industry processes (billing, metering, field service, customer service), choose IS‑U.
  • If you enjoy complex charging, rating, and subscription models, choose BRIM.

From a career perspective:

  • IS‑U → easier transition, more openings, strong demand with S/4 migrations
  • BRIM → fewer roles, but higher specialization and strong long‑term value

Both paths can take you into a functional or techno‑functional role, depending on your interest.

Hope this helps you make an informed decision.

entry.title: Re: Security deposit interest document creation from invoice
entry.content:
Hi,

You’re correct that Event 2015 (Interest Calculation: Create Document) is the right enhancement point, but as you’ve noticed, it fires for all interest‑creation scenarios — mass billing, FPINTM2/FPI2, and any custom program. So using it directly will stop interest creation everywhere, not just during the mass invoice run.

To control this only for the mass invoice job execution, you need a way to differentiate the calling process. Here are a few practical options based on my best understanding that are commonly used in IS‑U projects:

  1. Identify the Mass Billing Run Using the Billing Schema / Invoicing Context

During mass billing, the system sets specific runtime parameters (e.g., invoicing run ID, schema, or job name). You can read these in Event 2015 and conditionally skip interest creation only when the call originates from the mass billing process. Batch date and BATCH ID from EA38.

This is the cleanest approach because it avoids touching other processes.

  1. Use a Custom Switch (Z‑Parameter) Read by Event 2015

If your mass invoice job is triggered via a custom wrapper or variant, you can set a custom flag (e.g., in memory, TVARVC, or a Z‑table) before the run starts.

Event 2015 checks the flag → skips interest creation → resets after job completion.

This gives you precise control without impacting FPINTM2/FPI2.

  1. Check the Calling Program / Sy‑CPROG

Mass billing typically runs under specific programs such as:

  • REAINV00
  • REABILLB
  • REAINV01 (depending on version)

Inside Event 2015, you can check sy-cprog or cl_abap_get_call_stack to determine whether the call originated from the mass invoice run.

If yes → suppress interest creation If no → allow normal behavior

  1. Use a BAdI Instead of Event 2015 (If on S/4HANA)

if nothing work than the last option,  In S/4HANA, some customers prefer using:

  • BADI_FKK_SE_INT_CALC
  • BADI_FKK_SE_DOC_CREATE

These give more granular control and are easier to manage than classic events.

Hope this help, if not let me know, will try to see since I did similar in my project.

entry.title: Re: Configure based on Customer types
entry.content:
Hi,

Here’s how these scenarios are typically handled in IS‑U/AMI integrations:

  • Excluding customers with errors or zero‑consumption billing This is usually controlled in the MDUS/AMI extraction logic. Most utilities use a filtering step (either in the AMI middleware, MDUS BAdIs, or a custom selection program) to skip:
  1.  contracts with billing errors
  2. zero‑consumption bills
  3. accounts in dunning or special statuses You simply add these conditions in the selection criteria before generating the AMI request.
  • Sending only on‑cycle customers to AMI This is done by checking the billing class / billing cycle during the extraction. On‑cycle contracts are selected, and off‑cycle contracts are excluded. Many customers implement this via MDUS BAdIs or a custom wrapper around the AMI request creation.
  • Handling demand meter changes mid‑month When a demand meter is exchanged mid‑period, the system typically uses periodic register reads and selects the highest demand value across the old and new meters for that billing period. This is standard behavior in most MDUS/AMI integrations, but can also be controlled via custom logic if needed.

entry.title: Re: EA19 Document date check
entry.content:
Hi,

There’s no standard configuration in IS‑U to prevent users from entering a future Document Date in EA19. The transaction accepts any date as long as it’s a valid posting date in FI‑CA and this is very common requirement across all project I work.  its logically correct as well,  since we should not allow posting of financial document for future date since in between the timeframe anything happen like posting of payment or cancellation.  

If you need to enforce this rule, the usual approach is to implement a small check in the billing events, most commonly:

  • Event R402 / R403 (Billing document creation checks), or

  • Event 2000 / 2015 (FI‑CA document creation checks), depending on where you want the validation to happen.

Inside the event, you can compare the entered document date with sy-datum and raise an error if it’s greater than today.

There’s no standard switch or config for this, so an event enhancement is the recommended way.

Now the great Q is how do you handle your ORT/PT business cases, for those type of cases please apply a check in TVARVC, if that's check is enable update the date to current else keep it same. 

Hope this helps.

entry.title: Re: Automate BPEM Case Resolution Using Standard Configuration
entry.content:
Hi,

Standard SAP does not provide a way to automatically resolve BPEM cases end‑to‑end without any user action. I know it differ from previous response but still please read why I said so.

BPEM is intentionally designed as an exception‑handling framework, so SAP expects either a user decision or a custom automation layer (BAdIs, events, or external workflow) to close cases. Out‑of‑the‑box, there is no configuration switch that auto‑resolves cases created by EMMA.

Regarding EMMAJP → Execute Solution Processes: This button triggers the Solution Process assigned to the BPEM case category. A Solution Process is essentially a predefined follow‑up action (e.g., trigger a workflow, run a program, call a function module). It must be configured in:

IMG → SAP Utilities → Customer Service → BPEM → Define Solution Processes

If no solution process is assigned to the case category, the button will not perform any action. If a process is assigned, EMMAJP can execute it for multiple cases in mass.

To summarize:

  • Auto‑closing BPEM cases is not possible via standard; it requires enhancement.
  • “Execute Solution Processes” runs the configured follow‑up action for the case category.
  • You can maintain these processes in the BPEM configuration under Solution Processes.

Hope this helps. Let me know if you need the config path or BadI names, Basically auto closure rule is key since its setup for each case category individually.

entry.title: Re: How to navigate from RFKK_MA_SCHEDULER to FQEVENTS
entry.content:
Hi,

This is expected behavior. When you run EA26 directly, the billing program calls the FI‑CA event framework in dialog mode, so your breakpoint in Event R401 is hit immediately.

However, when you trigger the same process through RFKK_MA_SCHEDULER, the event is executed in a background job, not in the foreground session you are debugging. Because of that, your normal breakpoints will never be reached.

To analyze R401 during a mass invoice job, you have a few options:

  1. Use an external breakpoint (user‑specific) Set an external breakpoint in your R401 FM and make sure the background job runs under your user ID. Only external breakpoints are respected in background processing.
  2. Switch the job to run in “Debug Mode” In SM37, select the job → Job → “Debugging” before it starts. This forces the job to run in debug mode and will stop at your breakpoints.
  3. Insert a temporary hard stop As a last resort, add a temporary BREAK-POINT ID <id> or MESSAGE … inside R401 to force the debugger to open when the background job hits that code.
  4. Confirm that your variant actually triggers the same billing program RFKK_MA_SCHEDULER only schedules the mass activity. The actual billing is executed by the underlying mass activity program (e.g., REAINV00). Make sure your variant is correctly linked to EA26’s mass activity.

In summary: Your event is being triggered, but because the mass run executes in background, your normal breakpoints won’t fire. Use external breakpoints or debug the job directly to trace the call into R401.

entry.title: Re: Need help understanding meter reading reason 11 and 16
entry.content: Reason 11 is typically created as a pre‑programming meter read, while Reason 16 is generated after the programming activity is completed. Because the system records the initial read first and the confirmation read afterward, you usually see Reason 11 entries before Reason 16 in the meter‑reading sequence.

entry.title: Re: due date for credit memos FKK_CREATE_DOC
entry.content: When you use FKK_CREATE_DOC, the system treats credit memos as incoming payments from an FI‑CA perspective. Because of that, the due‑date logic follows the rules for receivables, not payables. Credit terms for outgoing payments are not evaluated here. This is standard FI‑CA behavior, not a limitation of the function module.

entry.title: Re: How do you manipulate the values of STXH, COBL, VBPA...
entry.content:
During VA01(Sales Order creation) there is no single user-exit to directly manipulate STXH, COBAL and VBPA together.

  1. VBAP - User save related exits in MV45AFZZ (like USEREXIT_SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE or USEREXIT_SAVE_DOCUMENT) and modify partner buffer table (XVBPA).
  2. COBL - Normally handled through configuration (OKB9 /derivation rules). If enhancement is required, user FI substitution or BADI. It is not purely controlled in SD exits.
  3. STXH: Use text determination or handle in MV45AFZZ save exit before the document is saved.

Always check configuration first before using exits. 

Regards,

Meenakshi.

entry.title: Re: VA01 Item Overview Sorting
entry.content: In standard VA01, the item overview is always sorted by POSNR. You cannot change the sorting to another VBAP field through configuration.

entry.title: Re: ABAP : Compiled language or Interpreted language
entry.content: ABAP is compiled to Program Code and then interpreted by the SAP runtime system.

entry.title: Securing SAP Agentic AI Across Architectural Boundaries
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as APJ Principal Cybersecurity and Compliance Advisor. As part of his responsibilities, Jana helps with strategic customer engagements related to topics such as AI Security & governance, cloud security, data privacy, secure data flow, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit and compliance.)

Introduction

 Enterprise AI is entering a new phase, shifting from deterministic automation to agentic autonomy, where AI systems can reason, plan, and execute multi-step workflows with minimal human intervention. In the SAP ecosystem, this evolution is enabled by SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and the SAP AI Core runtime, which together provide a scalable, tenant-isolated, hyperscaler-agnostic foundation for running agentic capabilities in an enterprise context.

These agents increasingly operate as digital coworkers, making real-time decisions, retrieving context, and interacting with business-critical processes and enterprise data. But with greater autonomy comes a new security reality: exposure to prompt injection, goal misalignment, hallucinations cascading into actions, privilege misuse, and tool-driven execution risks that traditional application security models were not designed to address. This blog walks through a secure, end-to-end architecture for SAP Agentic AI, highlighting the key control points needed to make autonomy both powerful and defensible.

This blog follows the end-to-end request lifecycle of an SAP Agentic AI interaction from the moment a user submits a query to the final audit trail. Each of the nine steps maps to a specific architectural layer, with its risks, data handling, and compliance controls examined inline. Think of it as walking alongside a request as it flows through the system.

Secure Agentic AI Architecture Flow

Figure 1 shows the complete request lifecycle of an SAP Agentic AI interaction. Each numbered step represents a distinct architectural boundary with its own security controls, data handling rules, and compliance requirements. The flow follows a request from the moment a user submits a query through Fiori, Joule, or a custom agent, all the way through identity verification, AI processing, business execution, and finally the forensic audit trail.

The following sections provide a detailed write-up for each step. Each step includes a breakdown diagram showing the internal flow, components involved, risks controlled, and the data that transitions between layers.

Figure1: Secure End-to End Agentic AI Flow

Step 1: User Request — Entry Point

The lifecycle begins when a user interacts with SAP Fiori, Joule Copilot, or a custom/third-party application. At this stage, the request already carries identity context (JWT is typically established via OAuth/OIDC flows and passed to BTP apps). The primary risk here is impersonation or malicious input submission. Mitigation relies on strong authentication controls such as SSO, MFA, and encrypted transport (TLS 1.2/1.3). From the first interaction, identity integrity and session security are enforced to prevent unauthorized access.

Figure 2: User Request: Entry FlowFigure 2: User Request: Entry Flow

Step 2: Identity & Access Control — Zero-Trust Enforcement

Once the request enters the platform, SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS/IPS) and OAuth-based authorization validate the identity and enforce role-based access control. XSUAA issues scoped, short-lived JWT tokens to ensure least-privilege execution. The risk at this layer is privilege escalation or token misuse. Mitigation comes from RBAC, scoped JWTs, and strict principal propagation, ensuring the AI agent acts strictly within subset of the user’s permissions preventing Confused Deputy attacks and ambient authority.

NOTE: SAP is evolving toward IAS + AMS for centralized, attribute-based authorization, while XSUAA remains supported for existing deployments.

Figure 3: Zero-Trust Enforcement

Step 3: Gen AI Hub — Inbound Policy Check

 Before any model is invoked, the request passes through the Gen AI Hub’s inbound control layer. This stage governs prompts as code using a Prompt Registry, applies input filtering to mitigate prompt injection, and masks PII before external model interaction. The key risk here is injection or data exfiltration via manipulated instructions. Mitigation includes version-controlled prompts, sanitization, and privacy-preserving preprocessing to ensure only policy-compliant inputs reach inference.

                                

Figure 4: Inbound Policy Check

Step 4: Trusted Data Retrieval — Hybrid RAG Grounding

To reduce hallucination risk, the system retrieves contextual enterprise data using a hybrid RAG approach. This includes semantic retrieval via the HANA Cloud Vector Engine and structured reasoning via a Knowledge Graph. Retrieval is authorization-aware, enforced via HANA Cloud Row-Level Security (RLS), ensuring the AI only accesses user-permitted context. The main risks are hallucination and unauthorized data exposure. Mitigation relies on controlled retrieval pipelines and strict enforcement of access controls during context assembly.

Figure 5: Hybrid RAG Grounding

Step 5: LLM Inference — SAP AI Core Runtime

Model inference executes inside SAP AI Core, which provides tenant-isolated runtime environments within SAP AI Core, using resource group segmentation based on Kubernetes namespaces. This prevents cross-tenant leakage and isolates workloads, while ensuring customer data is never used for foundation model training. Risks at this stage include model bias, model integrity concerns, or unintended data leakage. Mitigation is achieved through runtime isolation, encryption in transit and at rest, and enterprise-grade infrastructure controls. The runtime handles thinking — not governance or execution.

Figure 6: SAP AI Core Runtime

 Step 6: Gen AI Hub — Outbound Response Validation

After inference, responses pass back through the Gen AI Hub for post-processing. This stage applies output filtering, consistency validation against grounding sources, and controlled PII reconstruction for authorized recipients. The primary risk is cascading hallucination or unsafe output propagation. Mitigation includes policy filters and confidence checks that prevent unreliable outputs from triggering downstream actions or misleading users.

Figure 7: Outbound Response Validation

Step 7: MCP Gateway — Execution Boundary

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) Gateway, running within SAP Integration Suite/API Management, acts as the execution boundary between AI reasoning and business system action. It enforces tool allow-lists, per-tool authorization checks, and optional Human-in-the-Loop approval for sensitive operations. The key risk here is unauthorized transactions or tool misuse. Mitigation is achieved through scoped JWT enforcement, tool-level authorization validation, and workflow-based approvals before any system change occurs. This is relatively new capability reflecting SAP's evolving architecture, as MCP adoption is still early-stage in enterprise deployments.

Figure 8: MCP Gateway

Step 8: Backend Execution — Enterprise Systems

Once authorized, the action is executed via Standard APIs or Business Events in systems like SAP S/4HANA, adhering to Clean-Core principles and existing ERP controls. This stage operates under existing ERP controls and clean-core principles. The primary risks are financial misstatement, operational disruption, or regulatory breaches. Mitigation relies on established enterprise safeguards such as segregation of duties (SoD), transaction logging, and change management controls already embedded in core systems.

Figure 9: Backend Execution

Step 9:  Audit Logs — End-to-End Traceability

The final layer captures full traceability across the lifecycle: identity → proposal → decision → action → outcome. Audit logs are integrated with monitoring systems and retention policies to ensure forensic-grade investigation capability. The main risk at this stage is non-repudiation failure or investigation gaps. Mitigation comes from structured logging, monitoring, and evidence retention mechanisms that support regulatory and internal audit requirements.

Figure 10 : End to End Traceability

Data Processed across Layers

To complement the architectural flow, the table below maps each step of the SAP Agentic AI lifecycle to the type of data processed, what (if anything) is stored, the sensitivity classification, and the key controls applied. This view shifts the discussion from architecture to governance — clarifying how identity, prompts, embeddings, transactions, and audit traces are handled across the pipeline.

Rather than treating agentic AI as a single system, this breakdown highlights the distinct control layers: identity enforcement, orchestration governance, grounded inference, execution boundary controls, backend safeguards, and audit traceability. Understanding what data is processed and retained at each stage is critical for compliance, risk assessment, and regulatory alignment.

Step

Layer

Data processed

Data stored

Sensitivity

Key controls

1–2

Identity

User claims, JWTs

Session audit logs

PII

short-lived JWT

3

Inbound Gate

Prompts, PII (masked)

Prompt versions, filter logs

Business-Critical

PII masking, customer data is not used for 3rd party model training

4

Grounding

Embeddings, SPARQL queries

Vectors, ontologies, CDS

Confidential

Tenant isolation, auth-aware

5

LLM Inference

Sanitized prompts + context

None (stateless)

Masked

TLS 1.2+, customer data is not used for model training

6

Outbound Gate

Validated responses

Confidence logs

Business-Critical

Output filtering, PII reconstruction

7

MCP Gateway

Actions, tool params

Activity logs

Transactional

HITL, Audit Trail

8

Backend

ERP transactions

Transaction logs

Financial

ERP controls, SoD

9

Audit

Full event chain

BTP and Conversation Insight logs

Regulatory

Alignment to EU AI Act, GDPR,

Conclusion:

Securing agentic AI is not a single control — it is an architectural discipline. The nine-step SAP Agentic AI lifecycle shows why: identity enforcement, prompt governance, grounding, isolated inference, output validation, execution control, backend safeguards, and auditability each play a distinct role, and no layer can compensate for a missing one.

What makes the architecture defensible is how AI-specific safeguards compose with proven enterprise patterns: SAP Cloud Identity Services enforce zero-trust access, the Generative AI Hub orchestrates governed prompts, input/output controls and masking, grounding leverages HANA Cloud’s vector/semantic capabilities with authorization-aware retrieval, SAP AI Core provides tenant-isolated inference runtime under enterprise data protection terms, and the MCP Gateway via Integration Suite creates a policy-controlled execution boundary before any business action. The result is autonomy within enforceable trust boundaries, supporting auditability and governance expectations (e.g., GDPR, EU AI Act, ISO 42001) as organizations move from AI experimentation to controlled business process execution.

entry.title: Re: ABAP OOP
entry.content:
In real SAP projects, ABAP OOP is widely used, especially in S/4 HANA, RAP, FIORI and BADI Implementation.

  • Global classes are used for reusable business logic and framework (Preferred in real projects).
  • Local classes are used when the logic is specific to one report/program.

So, it depends on the requirement, but enterprise development mostly relies on global classes.

To level up, practice separating business logic into service classes and avoid logic directly in reports.

Best Regard,

Meenakshi.

entry.title: Color not updating in the Manage Production order fiori app

entry.title: Re: Introducing My Metrics: Your Personalized view of Metrics in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Hello @marouferchichi ,
Thanks for this insightful blog post on the new 'My Metrics' feature.

I'm currently working with an SAP Analytics Cloud story connected live to IBP. When I try to create a 'My Metric list' directly from a chart widget within the story,  I encounter the following message:

Could you please clarify if this is a known limitation when using 'My Metrics' with live data connections to IBP, or if there's a specific configuration required to enable this functionality?

Thanks in advance,
Regards,
swati

entry.title: Re: Mass migration of master data and fixed asset postings

entry.title: Re: WBS status is released and CRTD at the same time
entry.content: Hi Vaibhav, Thank you for your response. It is happening with specific WBS and I dont want to allow GR GI on CRTD status.

entry.title: Re: Mass migration of master data and fixed asset postings
entry.content:
Hi @SFerrand 

Update on the file size limitation:
The size limit for each uploaded XML file is 100 MB.  Since from 2602 version, you can upload multiple XML files at once by using a zip file. The size limit of the zip file is 100MB. The maximum size for each XML file in the zip file is 400MB.

Best regards,
Irene

entry.title: Re: Test data refresh SAP service prior to go live
entry.content: Hi @phaneste02, We marked the answer as accepted since there were no further updates on the topic after 7 days. If the answer provided was not helpful enough or you have further questions, you can unaccept it at any time. Best regards, Irene

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)
entry.content:
PLEASE NOTE: Selecting the ‘Will you be attending?’ option will NOT register you for the event.

To register for this event, please click here: Register Here

SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)

This session will help you understand the relevant components of an adequate adoption program to maximize your cloud investment and ensure sustained business value. We will walk you through the essentials to orchestrate Change Management, Governance, Sustainment, and Value Management and how they work together to drive ongoing adoption, solution optimization and user experience.​ Participants will explore best practices and take away actionable templates to build an initial transformation roadmap, define clear roles and responsibilities, and identify the right executive sponsors and stakeholder groups.​ This webcast is a foundational session that will lead into optional follow‑on topic specific webcasts on your preferred topics.. At the end, you will leave with a concrete structure and toolkit to start or refine your own SuccessFactors adoption program.

SuccessFactors Onboarding@sap.com for any questions on this event.

Here is a link to our Global schedule
